pathophysiology_summary: Scabies is infestation with the mite Sarcoptes scabiei, which burrows into the stratum corneum to lay eggs; a delayed hypersensitivity response to mites/eggs/faeces produces an intensely pruritic papular eruption that is characteristically worse at night, favouring finger webs, flexor wrists, axillae, periumbilical skin, and (in males) itchy scrotal/penile nodules, with head/neck sparing in adults. Diagnosis is supported by pathognomonic thread-like burrows and by symptomatic close contacts. Discriminators: burrows and scrotal nodules with an affected contact distinguish it from atopic dermatitis; the flexural/genital distribution with nocturnal itch and a contact case argues against contact dermatitis; and burrows/genital nodules argue against bedbug bites (grouped bites on exposed skin). Management is a topical scabicide (permethrin 5%) applied neck-to-toe (including webs/navel/genitals) for 8–14 h with a mandatory repeat at 7 days, SIMULTANEOUS treatment of all household/close contacts (even if asymptomatic), hot-washing of bedding/clothing, itch relief (sedating antihistamine), and clear counselling that post-scabetic itch persists 2–4 weeks (do not over-apply permethrin). Must-not-miss: secondary bacterial impetiginisation (raw skin) and crusted (Norwegian) scabies (hyperkeratotic, highly contagious, immunosuppressed) — both screened negative here.

differential.evidence_basis: Nocturnal itch + finger-web burrows + scrotal nodules + affected spouse.
Itchy but no burrows/scrotal nodules or a contagious contact.
Not typically nocturnal, flexural/genital with a contact case.
Grouped bites on exposed skin, not burrows/genital nodules.
Raw excoriated skin can superinfect — screen for crusts/spread/fever.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Diagnose scabies clinically, screen for secondary infection and crusted scabies, and treat the whole household simultaneously with a repeat at 7 days.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic triad; no secondary infection or crusted variant.
Not established now; remains a safety-net given raw skin.
Working diagnosis: permethrin (repeat at 7 days), simultaneous household treatment, hot-washing, itch relief; secondary-infection safety-net.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial impetiginisation/infection of excoriated skin (honey crusts, spreading hot redness, fever)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Raw skin can superinfect and progress to cellulitis (and rarely post-strep complications).

red_flags.description: Crusted (Norwegian) scabies — widespread hyperkeratotic crusts (highly contagious; suggests immunosuppression)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Needs specialist oral therapy (ivermectin) and infection-control measures.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not treating all household/close contacts simultaneously, causing reinfestation.
Repeating the cream nightly for the persistent post-scabetic itch, damaging the skin.
Not screening raw skin for infection or for the crusted variant.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prescribe permethrin 5% (neck-to-toe, repeat at 7 days), treat all contacts simultaneously, hot-wash linen, provide itch relief and post-scabetic-itch counselling; urgent review if secondary infection or crusted variant.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Treating only the patient (reinfestation) or missing secondary infection/crusted scabies.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Scabies: topical permethrin 5% applied to the whole body, repeated in 7 days; treat all close contacts simultaneously; launder bedding/clothing; oral ivermectin for crusted scabies or treatment failure.
